title: Homemade custard creams
subtitle: Vanilla biscuits with a custard-powder buttercream
excerpt: Custard creams in the British biscuit-tin tradition: a vanilla shortbread biscuit sandwiched with a buttercream flavoured with custard powder. Sixteen sandwiched biscuits.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the vanilla shortbread biscuit recipe and the buttercream filling appear in the cakes-and-biscuits chapter. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): documents the custard-cream sandwich biscuit as a Victorian household standard. Bird's custard powder (commercialised 1837, formula in public-domain trade literature) is the canonical filling flavour.

recipe.freezeNotes: Sandwich, freeze in a sealed tin layered with baking paper; defrost at room temperature.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bake the biscuits a day ahead; fill on the day of serving.

recipeTools.notes: 5 cm rectangle or oval, or a round.

content.text: Custard creams are a British biscuit-tin standard from the late nineteenth century. The biscuit is a creamed-butter vanilla shortbread; the filling is a buttercream stiffened with custard powder. The custard powder is the point: it gives the buttercream its pale yellow colour and the soft vanilla-and-cornflour flavour you remember from a packet biscuit.
If you do not have custard powder, an alternative is vanilla buttercream with 30 g of cornflour stirred through; the texture comes out close.

Beat the first 175 g of {{unsalted-butter}} with the {{caster-sugar}} until pale and creamed. Beat in the {{vanilla-extract}}. Sift in the {{plain-flour}}, first 25 g of {{cornflour}} and {{sea-salt-fine}} and bring together by hand to a soft dough. Press between two sheets of baking paper and chill flat for an hour. The cornflour gives a softer, more melt-in-the-mouth biscuit than plain flour alone.

Heat the oven to 170°C fan. Roll the chilled dough between the paper sheets to about 5 mm thick. Cut 32 biscuits with a 5 cm rectangular or oval cutter, pressing straight down. Set on lined trays with 2 cm between each. Bake for 12–14 minutes; the biscuits should be pale gold all over with set edges. Cool on the trays for 5 minutes, then on a rack.

Beat the second 100 g of {{unsalted-butter}} with the {{icing-sugar}}, second 30 g of {{cornflour}} and {{vanilla-bean-paste}} until pale and smooth. Pipe or spread onto the flat side of half the cooled biscuits; sandwich with the remaining biscuits. The filling firms up over the first hour at room temperature.
